title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created

input_row.title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created
input_row.description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server. The --audit- policy-file flag must be set for this logging to be enabled.
input_row.rationale: Logging is an important detective control for all systems, to detect potential unauthorised access. Impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on one of the cluster master nodes: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --audit-policy-file is set. Review the contents of the file specified and ensure that it contains a valid audit policy.
input_row.remediation: Create an audit policy file for your cluster. Default Value: Unless the --audit-policy-file flag is specified, no auditing will be carried out. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/
input_row.impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control requires verifying that the Kubernetes API server is started with the --audit-policy-file flag set, and that the referenced file exists and is a valid audit policy.
- The presence and value of API server flags can be queried via Kubernetes APIs (e.g., by inspecting the kube-apiserver Pod spec in static Pod clusters or via the API server deployment in managed clusters).
- No subjective human review or in-guest OS access is required for flag presence; the audit policy file content validity (syntax) can be checked by automation for existence and basic structure.
- Therefore, this check is fully automatable.

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_api_server_audit_policy_file_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Query the kube-apiserver configuration to verify the --audit-policy-file flag is set.
    - For static Pod clusters: Use kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml to inspect the command/args of the kube-apiserver Pod.
    - For managed clusters: Query the API server configuration resource (if exposed) or the relevant deployment/statefulset.
- Parse the command/args field to confirm the presence of --audit-policy-file=<path>.
- Optionally, check that the referenced file path is mounted into the Pod via a volumeMount.
- (Optional advanced check) If the file is accessible (e.g., via hostPath or ConfigMap), retrieve its contents and verify it is a valid Kubernetes audit policy (YAML with required fields such as apiVersion, kind: Policy, rules).
- Pass Criteria:
    - The kube-apiserver is started with the --audit-policy-file flag set to a valid file path.
    - (Optional) The file exists and is a valid Kubernetes audit policy.
- Fail Criteria:
    - The --audit-policy-file flag is missing or empty.
    - (Optional) The referenced file does not exist or is not a valid audit policy.
- Example kubectl command:
    - kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}'
    - Or, for args: kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].args}'
    - Parse output for --audit-policy-file flag.
